revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/85902712|日向三代]]"
Reference provided but also question notability and if this shouldn't be merged with the Amaterasu article.
Added {{[[Template:Merge to|Merge to]]}} tag: Recommend merging
Immanuelle moved page [[Three generations of Hyuga]] to [[Template:Three generations of Hyuga]]: attribution to template, template works better for it

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52 |access-date=2020-11-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}https://archive.today/20230406174104/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9716|boxstyle_002=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | |!| | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | |0012 |y|0022 | | |004 |0012=[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]|boxstyle_0012=background:lightblue;|0022={{Bgcolor||[[Takuhadachiji-hime]]}}{{Cite web |title=タクハタチヂヒメ |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/155.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=栲幡千千姫命（たくはたちぢひめのみこと）ご利益と神社 |url=https://xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia/shinto14/shrine88.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Ninigi |url=https://mythopedia.com/topics/ninigi |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=Mythopedia |language=en}}|boxstyle_0022=background:pink;|004=[[Ōyamatsumi]]''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52 |access-date=2020-11-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}https://archive.today/20230406174104/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9716|boxstyle_002=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | |!| | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | |0012 |y|0022 | | |004 |0012=[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]|boxstyle_0012=background:lightblue;|0022={{Bgcolor||[[Takuhadachiji-hime]]}}{{Cite web |title=タクハタチヂヒメ |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/155.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=栲幡千千姫命（たくはたちぢひめのみこと）ご利益と神社 |url=https://xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia/shinto14/shrine88.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Ninigi |url=https://mythopedia.com/topics/ninigi |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=Mythopedia |language=en}}|boxstyle_0022=background:pink;|004=[[Ōyamatsumi]]''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''
（天孫）|boxstyle_0013=background:lightblue;|0023=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor||Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0023=background:pink;|0032=[[Watatsumi]]{{cite journal |last1=Akima |first1=Toshio |date=1993 |title=The Origins of the Grand Shrine of Ise and the Cult of the Sun Goddess Amaterasu Ōmikami |journal=Japan Review |volume=4 |issue=4 |page=143 |issn=0915-0986 |jstor=25790929}}{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |access-date=2023-12-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 }}|boxstyle_0032=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| |,|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|(| | | |,|-|-|v|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|Hod | |0014 | | | | | | |0024 |y|0033 | |!| |0042 | |w004 |Hod=[[Hoderi]]|boxstyle_Hod=background:lightblue;|0014=[[Hosuseri]]

First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52 |access-date=2020-11-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}https://archive.today/20230406174104/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9716|boxstyle_002=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | |!| | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | |0012 |y|0022 | | |004 |0012=[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]]|boxstyle_0012=background:lightblue;|0022={{Bgcolor||[[Takuhadachiji-hime]]}}{{Cite web |title=タクハタチヂヒメ |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/155.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=栲幡千千姫命（たくはたちぢひめのみこと）ご利益と神社 |url=https://xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia/shinto14/shrine88.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Ninigi |url=https://mythopedia.com/topics/ninigi |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=Mythopedia |language=en}}|boxstyle_0022=background:pink;|004=[[Ōyamatsumi]]''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''
（天孫）|boxstyle_0013=background:lightblue;|0023=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor||Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0023=background:pink;|0032=[[Watatsumi]]{{cite journal |last1=Akima |first1=Toshio |date=1993 |title=The Origins of the Grand Shrine of Ise and the Cult of the Sun Goddess Amaterasu Ōmikami |journal=Japan Review |volume=4 |issue=4 |page=143 |issn=0915-0986 |jstor=25790929}}{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |access-date=2023-12-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 }}|boxstyle_0032=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| |,|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|(| | | |,|-|-|v|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|Hod | |0014 | | | | | | |0024 |y|0033 | |!| |0042 | |w004 |Hod=[[Hoderi]]|boxstyle_Hod=background:lightblue;|0014=[[Hosuseri]]
（海幸彦）|boxstyle_0014=background:lightblue;|0024='''[[Hoori]]'''{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}
（山幸彦）|boxstyle_0024=background:lightblue;|0033=[[Toyotama-hime|{{Bgcolor||Toyotama-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0033=background:pink;|0042={{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|qq=draft}}{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}|boxstyle_0042=background:lightblue;|w004={{ill|Furutama-no-Mikoto|ja|布留多摩命|qq=draft}}|boxstyle_w004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | |!| | |!| | |!}} {{familytree|Tens | | |!| | | | | | | | | |0025 |y|~|0034 |0043 | |w0042|Tens={{ill|Tensori no Mikoto|ja|天曽利命|qq=draft}}|boxstyle_Tens=background:lightblue;|0025='''[[Ugayafukiaezu]]'''{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oNptW22CSqMC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=%22Itsuse%22&q=%22Itsuse%22&hl=en |title=The History of Nations: Japan. Dept. of education. Japan |date=1910 |publisher=H. W. Snow |language=en}}|boxstyle_0025=background:lightblue;|0034=[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|{{Bgcolor||Tamayori-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0034=background:pink;|0043={{Background color||[[Azumi people]]}}|boxstyle_0043=background:lightgreen;|w0042=（{{Background color||{{ill|Owari clan|lt=Owari clan|ja|尾張国造}}

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}|boxstyle_Ahira=background:pink;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |!| |`|-|.}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |0017 | |Tag |0017={{Background color||[[Imperial House of Japan]]}}|boxstyle_0017=background:lightgreen;|Tag=[[Tagishimimi]]{{cite book|author=Norinaga Motoori|title=The Poetics of Motoori Norinaga: A Hermeneutical Journey|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Zk4EucRlvSwC&pg=PA191|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3078-6|page=191}}{{cite book|author=Gary L. Ebersole|title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pdv_oyEq4VQC&pg=PA108|year=1992|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-01929-0|pages=108–109}}{{cite book|title=The Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jf_PAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT218|date=19 June 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0511-9|page=218}} |boxstyle_Tag=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree|003=（[[天津神|天神]]）|boxstyle_003=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree/end}}
* {{Background color|#F2CEE0|Red background}} is female. * {{Background color|#00FF00|Green background}} means groups * Bold letters are three generations of Hyuga.

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''
（天孫）|boxstyle_0013=background:lightblue;|0023=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor||Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0023=background:pink;|0032=[[Watatsumi]]{{cite journal |last1=Akima |first1=Toshio |date=1993 |title=The Origins of the Grand Shrine of Ise and the Cult of the Sun Goddess Amaterasu Ōmikami |journal=Japan Review |volume=4 |issue=4 |page=143 |issn=0915-0986 |jstor=25790929}}{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Futodama |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=52 |access-date=2020-11-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}https://archive.today/20230406174104/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9716|boxstyle_002=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | |!| | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | |0012 |y|0022 | | |004 |0012=[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]]|boxstyle_0012=background:lightblue;|0022={{Bgcolor||[[Takuhadachiji-hime]]}}{{Cite web |title=タクハタチヂヒメ |url=https://nihonsinwa.com/page/155.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=nihonsinwa.com |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=栲幡千千姫命（たくはたちぢひめのみこと）ご利益と神社 |url=https://xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia/shinto14/shrine88.html |access-date=2023-01-17 |website=xn--u9ju32nb2az79btea.asia |language=ja}}{{Cite web |title=Ninigi |url=https://mythopedia.com/topics/ninigi |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=Mythopedia |language=en}}|boxstyle_0022=background:pink;|004=[[Ōyamatsumi]]''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''
（天孫）|boxstyle_0013=background:lightblue;|0023=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor||Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0023=background:pink;|0032=[[Watatsumi]]{{cite journal |last1=Akima |first1=Toshio |date=1993 |title=The Origins of the Grand Shrine of Ise and the Cult of the Sun Goddess Amaterasu Ōmikami |journal=Japan Review |volume=4 |issue=4 |page=143 |issn=0915-0986 |jstor=25790929}}{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |access-date=2023-12-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 }}|boxstyle_0032=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| |,|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|(| | | |,|-|-|v|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|Hod | |0014 | | | | | | |0024 |y|0033 | |!| |0042 | |w004 |Hod=[[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]]|boxstyle_Hod=background:lightblue;|0014=[[Hosuseri]]
（海幸彦）|boxstyle_0014=background:lightblue;|0024='''[[Hoori]]'''{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}

Snow |language=en}}|boxstyle_0025=background:lightblue;|0034=[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|{{Bgcolor||Tamayori-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0034=background:pink;|0043={{Background color||[[Azumi people]]}}|boxstyle_0043=background:lightgreen;|w0042=（{{Background color||{{ill|Owari clan|lt=Owari clan|ja|尾張国造}}

Ebersole|title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pdv_oyEq4VQC&pg=PA108|year=1992|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-01929-0|pages=108–109}}{{cite book|title=The Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jf_PAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT218|date=19 June 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0511-9|page=218}} |boxstyle_Tag=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree|003=（[[天津神|天神]]）|boxstyle_003=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree/end}}

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''
（天孫）|boxstyle_0013=background:lightblue;|0023=[[Konohanasakuya-hime|{{Bgcolor||Konohanasakuya-hime}}]]|boxstyle_0023=background:pink;|0032=[[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]{{cite journal |last1=Akima |first1=Toshio |date=1993 |title=The Origins of the Grand Shrine of Ise and the Cult of the Sun Goddess Amaterasu Ōmikami |journal=Japan Review |volume=4 |issue=4 |page=143 |issn=0915-0986 |jstor=25790929}}{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |access-date=2023-12-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 }}|boxstyle_0032=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| |,|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|(| | | |,|-|-|v|-|-|+|-|-|.}} {{familytree|Hod | |0014 | | | | | | |0024 |y|0033 | |!| |0042 | |w004 |Hod=[[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]]|boxstyle_Hod=background:lightblue;|0014=[[Hosuseri]]

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''

Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign [[:en:Bedroom|bower]] and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women."|boxstyle_004=background:lightblue;}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | | | | | |!}} {{familytree| | | | | | | | | | | |0013 |y|~|~|~|0023 | | |0032 |0013='''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]'''
